itinerary_title: 東京夏夜花火浪漫之旅 — Five-Day Japan Fireworks Journey, Late July 2027
primary_event: Sumida River Fireworks Festival (隅田川花火大会) — Saturday, July 24, 2027

accommodation.why: Ground zero for the Sumida River Fireworks; walkable to Senso-ji, Skytree, and the river viewing areas. The old-town atmosphere is far more romantic than Shinjuku or Shibuya for a couple's trip.

primary_recommendation.name: The Gate Hotel Kaminarimon by HULIC
primary_recommendation.style: Modern luxury boutique with rooftop terrace overlooking Senso-ji and Skytree
primary_recommendation.why: The rooftop bar/lounge has an unobstructed Skytree view. On fireworks night you can watch from the terrace if you skip the crowds.

alternative_recommendation.name: Cyashitsu Ryokan Asakusa
alternative_recommendation.style: Traditional ryokan with private rooftop onsen and Skytree views
alternative_recommendation.why: Intimate, traditional atmosphere. The private rooftop hot tub under the Skytree at night is an unforgettable couples' moment.

days.title: 初逢東都・夏風輕拂
days.title_translation: First Encounter with the Eastern Capital — A Gentle Summer Breeze

morning.activity: Arrive at Narita or Haneda. Take Keisei Skyliner or Limousine Bus to Asakusa. Check in at The Gate Hotel Kaminarimon.
morning.detail: Drop bags, freshen up. Walk to Kaminarimon Gate — the iconic red lantern. Arrive before 10 AM to photograph the gate without crowds.

afternoon.activity: Yukata rental at Kimono Rental Yae (着物レンタル八重), then explore Nakamise-dori and Senso-ji Temple
afternoon.detail: Book the Couple's Kimono Package in advance. Dress in summer yukata together and stroll the 250m Nakamise shopping street. Visit Senso-ji's main hall, draw an omikuji fortune together. Continue to the five-story pagoda for photos.
afternoon.venue_address: Yae: 1-16-2 Asakusa, Taito-ku (1 min from Asakusa Station)

evening.activity: Sunset walk along the Sumida River promenade, then dinner
evening.detail: Walk south along the river toward Azumabashi Bridge. The Skytree lights up at dusk — the reflection on the water is stunning. This is the same stretch where the fireworks will explode on Day 3, so you're scouting your spots.

restaurant.name: Sometaro (染太郎)
restaurant.what_to_order: The house monjayaki (Tokyo's answer to Osaka's okonomiyaki) and a seafood okonomiyaki. Cook it together on the table grill — it's interactive and romantic.
restaurant.note: This shop has been operating since 1937. The old wooden interior is full of character. Cash preferred.

days.secret_moment: After dinner, walk to the tiny Bentendo Hall on the island in Shinobazu Pond (a 15-min walk or short taxi to Ueno). The lotus flowers bloom massively in July, and the temple is lit at night. You'll have the moment nearly to yourselves.

days.title: 古都碧波・鎌倉尋幽
days.title_translation: Azure Waves of the Ancient Capital — Seeking Serenity in Kamakura

morning.activity: Train to Kamakura (JR Yokosuka Line from Tokyo Station, ~60 min). Visit Hokoku-ji Temple (報国寺) — the Bamboo Temple
morning.detail: Arrive by 9:30 AM. Take bus #5 from Kamakura Station east exit to Jomyoji (浄明寺). Hokoku-ji's bamboo grove has 2,000+ moso bamboo stalks. Pay ¥500 to enter the tea house inside the grove and drink matcha surrounded by bamboo. Far less crowded than Kyoto's Arashiyama.

afternoon.activity: Visit the Great Buddha (Kotoku-in), then walk the Hase area. End at Yuigahama Beach.
afternoon.detail: The 13m bronze Amida Buddha has sat here since 1252. From Hase Station, walk to Hase-dera Temple for panoramic ocean views from the observation deck. Then descend to Yuigahama Beach — spread a towel and watch surfers. On clear days, Mt. Fuji is visible across Sagami Bay.

evening.activity: Sunset at Inamuragasaki Point, then return to Tokyo
evening.detail: Walk or take the charming Enoden train one stop from Hase to Inamuragasaki. The rocky headland is one of the Kanto region's most famous sunset spots — on clear evenings, the sun sets directly behind Enoshima Island with Mt. Fuji silhouetted. Take the 7 PM Enoden back to Kamakura, then JR to Tokyo.

restaurant.name: Matsubara-an (松原庵)
restaurant.cuisine: Artisan soba noodles in a converted seaside villa
restaurant.what_to_order: Ten-seiro (cold soba with tempura). The soba is hand-cut daily. Pair with a glass of cold junmai sake.
restaurant.note: The restaurant is in a beautiful old Japanese house with a garden. Lunch service is best; arrive before noon to avoid waits.

days.secret_moment: At Hase-dera Temple, find the small cave (Benten-kutsu) carved into the hillside — inside are hundreds of tiny carved stone Benzaiten goddesses lit by candlelight. Most tourists walk right past it. Make a wish together inside the cave.

days.title: 隅田川上・萬花齊放
days.title_translation: Above the Sumida River — Ten Thousand Flowers Bloom at Once

morning.activity: Slow morning. Visit Yanaka district (谷中) — Tokyo's nostalgic old quarter
morning.detail: Take the train to JR Nippori Station. Walk the Yanaka Cemetery path (surprisingly beautiful — enormous old trees, Tokyo Skytree visible through the branches). Descend the famous Yuyake Dandan sunset staircase into Yanaka Ginza shopping street. Browse tiny craft shops, try menchi-katsu (fried meat croquette) from a street vendor.

afternoon.activity: Return to Asakusa. Prepare for fireworks. Claim a spot by 4 PM.
afternoon.detail: The Sumida River Fireworks draws 1,000,000+ spectators. Key strategy: the festival has two launch sites — Sakurabashi (First Area, upstream) and Komagatabashi (Second Area, downstream). The Second Area near Komagatabashi Bridge is slightly less insane. Lay a blue tarp (buy one at any convenience store or Daiso for ¥300). Bring konbini snacks, cold drinks, and a portable fan. Alternatively, book a paid seat in advance (¥7,000–10,000/person) for guaranteed views.
afternoon.pro_tip: If you want to skip the ground-level crush entirely: book a yakatabune (屋形船) — a traditional pleasure boat that floats on the river during the fireworks. Companies like Amitatsu (網辰) and Funatsuudon (船津丼) offer dinner cruises with tempura and sashimi, ¥15,000–25,000/person.

evening.activity: Sumida River Fireworks Festival (隅田川花火大会)
evening.detail: Approximately 20,000 fireworks shells launched from 7:00 PM to 8:30 PM. The two launch sites fire alternating volleys. The reflection on the Sumida River doubles every burst. The crowd gasps and calls 'Tamaya!' and 'Kagiya!' — the names of historic Edo-era fireworks makers. Wear your yukata from Day 1 (many rental shops offer multi-day plans).
viewing_spots_ranked: 1. Yakatabune boat on the river (most romantic, must book weeks ahead)
2. Tokyo Skytree Tembo Deck — 350m above, looking DOWN at the fireworks (book Skytree fireworks special tickets via en.tokyo-skytree.jp)
3. Sumida Park (both east and west banks near Azumabashi Bridge)
4. Komagatabashi Bridge area (Second Area — slightly less packed)
5. Rooftop of The Gate Hotel Kaminarimon (if staying there — ask at check-in)

restaurant.name: Konbini dinner picnic (this is intentional)
restaurant.neighborhood: Any Lawson, 7-Eleven, or FamilyMart near Asakusa
restaurant.cuisine: Japanese convenience store food — an underrated culinary genre
restaurant.what_to_order: Onigiri (salmon and umeboshi), karaage (fried chicken), edamame, a Suntory Premium Malt's beer and a canned highball. Total: ¥2,000 for two.
restaurant.note: On fireworks night, every restaurant within 2km of the river will be packed or closed early. The authentic move is a blue-tarp picnic by the river with konbini food. This is exactly what Japanese couples do. Lean into it.

days.secret_moment: After the last firework fades, don't rush to the station (the crush is brutal for 90+ minutes). Instead, walk east across Sakurabashi Bridge to the Skytree side. The tower is lit in special fireworks-night colors. Find the quiet Oshiage canal-side path — you'll have a moonlit walk with the illuminated tower overhead while a million people fight for trains behind you.

days.title: 下北澤巷・時光琥珀
days.title_translation: The Alleys of Shimokitazawa — Amber Trapped in Time

morning.activity: Sleep in after fireworks night. Late morning brunch at Shimokitazawa.
morning.detail: Take the Keio-Inokashira Line from Shibuya (3 min express) to Shimokitazawa Station. Start at Norah's Coffee Table for a slow pour-over coffee and toast. Then wander the vintage shops — Stick Out (all items ¥700), Flamingo, and New York Joe Exchange are the holy trinity of Shimokita thrifting.

afternoon.activity: Explore BONUS TRACK (ボーナストラック) and the reimagined Shimokitazawa rail corridor
afternoon.detail: BONUS TRACK is a micro-neighborhood built on the former Odakyu rail line — independent bookshops, craft beer taprooms, a record shop, and artisan food stalls in long, low timber buildings. Then walk the connected Shimokita Ekiue development above the station. Visit the bookshop B&B for curated Japanese design books.

evening.activity: Head to Shibuya for the Scramble Crossing at dusk, then cocktails in Shinjuku
evening.detail: Watch the Shibuya Crossing from the second floor of the Shibuya Scramble Square building (free viewing). Then take the JR Yamanote Line to Shinjuku. Walk through Omoide Yokocho (Memory Lane / Piss Alley) — a narrow alley of tiny yakitori joints under a canopy of lanterns. End the night at Bar Ben Fiddich in Shinjuku — one of Asia's most acclaimed cocktail bars, where bartender Hiroyasu Kayama forages his own botanicals and makes bitters from scratch.

restaurant.name: Fuunji (風雲児)
restaurant.neighborhood: Shinjuku (south of Shinjuku Station, near Takashimaya Times Square)
restaurant.what_to_order: The signature tsukemen — thick noodles dipped in a rich, creamy tonkotsu-gyokai (pork and fish) broth. Add a soft-boiled egg. Get the large size (特盛) — same price.
restaurant.note: There will be a line. Join it. It moves fast (15–25 min). Buy tickets from the vending machine inside. This is consistently ranked among Tokyo's top 5 tsukemen shops.

days.secret_moment: At Bar Ben Fiddich (9F, Nishi-Shinjuku 1-13-7), there's no sign on the building — just a door and an elevator. The bar has only 10 seats. Kayama-san will ask what flavors you like and create a bespoke cocktail using herbs he grows on his family farm in Saitama. Order the absinthe cocktail made tableside with a hand-cranked distillation setup. This is a world-class experience that feels like a secret.

days.title: 離歌未央・東京殘夢
days.title_translation: The Parting Song Is Not Yet Over — Lingering Dreams of Tokyo

morning.activity: Early visit to Tsukiji Outer Market (築地場外市場)
morning.detail: Arrive by 8 AM. While the inner wholesale market moved to Toyosu, the outer market's 400+ food stalls and shops remain. Must-try: fresh tamagoyaki (sweet rolled omelette) from Yamacho (山長), uni (sea urchin) on a stick, and a sashimi breakfast bowl at Sushi Dai or Daiwa Sushi in the outer market area.

afternoon.activity: Luxury shopping and gallery stroll in Ginza, then farewell matcha at Higashiya Ginza
afternoon.detail: Walk from Tsukiji to Ginza (15 min). Browse the Ginza Six department store's art installations on B1 (Tsutaya Books' art book floor is stunning). Visit Itoya (伊東屋) — a 12-story stationery paradise. End at Higashiya Ginza (ひがしや銀座) for a formal wagashi (Japanese confection) and matcha service. Each sweet is a miniature edible sculpture.

evening.activity: Depart from Narita or Haneda, or extend for one more night
evening.detail: If flying late, take the Keisei Skyliner from Ueno to Narita (36 min) or monorail from Hamamatsucho to Haneda (13 min). Allow 3 hours total for airport transit and check-in.

restaurant.name: Higashiya Ginza (ひがしや銀座)
restaurant.cuisine: Modern Japanese tea salon and wagashi
restaurant.what_to_order: The seasonal namagashi set with matcha — in July they make wagashi shaped like goldfish, morning glories, and flowing water. Also try the warabi mochi (bracken-starch jelly with kinako powder).
restaurant.note: This is the most elegant farewell to Japan you can create. The interior is designed by Shinichiro Ogata (master of Japanese spatial aesthetics). Reservations recommended.

days.secret_moment: Before leaving Tsukiji, find the tiny Namiyoke Inari Shrine (波除稲荷神社) at the market's edge. It's the guardian shrine of Tsukiji, with a massive lion head (shishi-gashira) at the entrance. Buy a small food-themed omamori (protective charm) — they have ones specifically for 'good cooking' and 'protection from food allergies,' which are charmingly specific souvenirs.

backup_fireworks_festivals.name: Katsushika Noryo Fireworks Festival (葛飾納涼花火大会)
backup_fireworks_festivals.location: Shibamata Baseball Ground, near Edogawa River
backup_fireworks_festivals.access: 10 min walk from Keisei Shibamata Station
backup_fireworks_festivals.why_great: Much closer launch distance than Sumida — the fireworks feel like they're exploding directly above you. The nearby Shibamata Taishakuten Temple and its retro shopping street are worth visiting beforehand. A drone show collaboration is planned for recent editions.

backup_fireworks_festivals.name: Edogawa Fireworks Festival (江戸川区花火大会)
backup_fireworks_festivals.access: Toei Shinjuku Line to Shinozaki Station
backup_fireworks_festivals.why_great: Famous for its opening salvo — 1,000 fireworks launched in 5 seconds, creating an overwhelming wall of light. Held simultaneously with the Ichikawa Fireworks Festival on the Chiba side of the river, so you get double the show.

backup_fireworks_festivals.name: Itabashi Fireworks Festival (いたばし花火大会)
backup_fireworks_festivals.location: Arakawa River near Toda Bridge, Itabashi-ku
backup_fireworks_festivals.access: JR Saikyo Line to Ukimafunado Station, or Toei Mita Line to Takashimadaira
backup_fireworks_festivals.why_great: Features Japan's largest niagara-style firework (a 700m cascading waterfall of light). Simultaneously held with the Todashi Fireworks on the Saitama side. Less international-tourist-heavy than Sumida.

food_5_days.note: Mix of konbini, ramen, mid-range izakaya, and 1-2 upscale meals

transport_local.note: Suica card loads, Kamakura day trip JR fare, Enoden, etc.

activities_and_experiences.includes: Yukata rental (couple plan ~¥7,000), temple admissions, Skytree tickets, fireworks paid seats, bar tabs

shopping_and_souvenirs.note: Varies wildly. Shimokitazawa vintage is cheap. Ginza is not.

total_estimate_two_people.note: Flights excluded. Yakatabune fireworks cruise adds ¥30,000–50,000 for two.

packing_essentials.climate_context: Late July Tokyo: 30–35°C (86–95°F), humidity 75–85%. The rainy season (tsuyu) typically ends around July 20, but sudden afternoon downpours remain common.
essentials: Lightweight, breathable clothing — linen shirts, cotton dresses, moisture-wicking underlayers
Compact UV-blocking folding umbrella (doubles as sun parasol — Japanese women carry these constantly)
Sunscreen SPF50+ (Japanese brands Biore UV Aqua Rich or Anessa are superior and available at any drugstore upon arrival)
Small hand towel / tenugui — Japanese summer essential for dabbing sweat (buy a pretty one at Nakamise-dori on Day 1)
Portable handheld fan (battery-powered neck fans are everywhere in Tokyo — buy on arrival at Daiso or Don Quijote for ¥500–1,500)
Light rain jacket or packable poncho for sudden showers
Comfortable walking sandals AND closed-toe walking shoes (temples sometimes require shoes off/on repeatedly)
Insect repellent patches (mosquitoes are active in July, especially near water and parks)
Reusable water bottle — refill at vending machines (¥100–160 per bottle)
For fireworks night: a small plastic ground sheet / picnic tarp, wet wipes, and a portable phone charger
what_not_to_bring: Heavy jeans or dark-colored clothes (you will overheat)
Large luggage for day trips (use coin lockers at stations, ¥300–700)
Excessive cash — Japan is increasingly card-friendly, but carry ¥30,000–50,000 in cash for small shops, shrines, and street food

suica_card.what: Rechargeable IC card for all trains, buses, and many vending machines/convenience stores
suica_card.where_to_get: Available on iPhone Wallet (add a Suica card digitally before departure) or buy a physical Welcome Suica at Narita/Haneda airport JR counters
suica_card.note: Physical Suica cards had supply shortages in 2023-2024 but Welcome Suica (tourist version, 28-day validity, no deposit) is available at airports. The iPhone Wallet Suica is the most convenient option — just tap your phone.

jr_lines.key_line: JR Yamanote Line — the green circle line hitting Shinjuku, Shibuya, Tokyo, Ueno, Nippori (for Yanaka), Akihabara. You'll use this daily.
jr_lines.kamakura: JR Yokosuka Line from Tokyo Station to Kamakura Station (~60 min, ¥950 one way). Covered by JR Pass but a pass is NOT cost-effective for a 5-day Tokyo-only trip.
jr_lines.airport: Keisei Skyliner (Narita to Ueno, 36 min, ¥2,520) or Keikyu Line (Haneda to Asakusa, ~35 min, ¥630)

pro_tips: Do NOT buy a JR Rail Pass for this itinerary — it costs ¥50,000+ and only saves money if you're taking shinkansen between cities
Use Google Maps for all train navigation — it shows real-time departures and platform numbers
Trains stop running at ~midnight and resume at ~5 AM. Last trains fill up fast on fireworks night — plan to walk or wait
Taxis are clean, safe, and metered but expensive (¥500 base fare + ¥100 per ~250m). Doors open automatically — don't pull them.
The Toei Subway and Tokyo Metro are separate systems but both accept Suica. Transfers between them cost more than staying on one system.

final_notes.fireworks_rain_policy: Sumida River Fireworks go ahead in light rain. They are postponed or canceled only for typhoons or severe storms. If canceled, there is NO standard reschedule date — the event is simply canceled for the year. This is why the backup festivals above are important.
final_notes.booking_advance_warning: Book the yakatabune and any fireworks paid seats by early June 2027 at the latest. Yukata rental for fireworks weekend books out 2–4 weeks ahead. The Gate Hotel and other Asakusa hotels near the river sell out months in advance for fireworks weekend.
final_notes.language: English is limited outside tourist hubs. Download Google Translate's offline Japanese pack. Learn: sumimasen (excuse me), arigatou gozaimasu (thank you), and oishi (delicious) — these three words will carry you far.
